nativeRange.habitat: Coastal Atlantic-drainage streams and rivers in southeastern Brazil (states of São Paulo and Rio de Janeiro), typically swift-flowing, well-oxygenated, clear to slightly turbid water over sand, gravel, or rocky substrate with leaf litter

decorPreferences: leaf litter (dried oak or beech leaves)

diet.notes: Benthic forager. Accepts high-quality sinking pellets, wafers, and catfish tablets as a staple. Relishes live and frozen foods: bloodworms, tubifex, daphnia, brine shrimp, and white worms. Will graze on biofilm and detritus. Feed after lights out to ensure this species competes effectively with faster tankmates.

goodWith: other cool-water South American fish (tetras, small characins)
Corydoras species with overlapping temperature ranges
non-aggressive dwarf cichlids tolerating cooler temperatures
avoidWith: tropical species requiring temperatures above 26°C (incompatible thermal range)
fast, competitive feeders that outcompete for sinking foods

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo for Scleromystax barbatus. Verify availability and licensing before use.

summary: The Barbatus Cory is the largest species in its genus group, reaching up to 12.5 cm. Unlike most corydorids it prefers cool, well-oxygenated water (18–24°C) reflecting its southeastern Brazilian coastal stream origin. Adult males develop distinctive bristle-like odontodes on the cheeks and body — a key sexing feature. Note: reclassified from Corydoras to Scleromystax by Britto (2003).
careNotes: Scleromystax barbatus is the largest species in the broader corydorad catfish group and was reclassified from Corydoras into the resurrected genus Scleromystax by Britto in 2003, based on morphological and phylogenetic distinctions. The genus Scleromystax is characterised by sexual dimorphism in which mature males develop odontodes (bristle-like dermal teeth) on the snout, operculum, and body flanks — absent in females — making sexing straightforward in adult fish. This species originates from fast-flowing, well-oxygenated Atlantic-drainage coastal streams in southeastern Brazil (São Paulo and Rio de Janeiro states), and its cool-water preference (18–24°C) is a critical husbandry point: sustained temperatures above 26°C cause stress, reduced lifespan, and susceptibility to disease. Standard tropical community heaters are typically unsuitable; a chiller or an unheated room-temperature setup is often ideal in temperate climates. Fine sand substrate is strongly recommended to prevent barbel erosion; coarse gravel causes abrasion injury. Keep in groups of five or more to promote natural shoaling and foraging behaviour. Good filtration providing steady current and high dissolved oxygen is appreciated. Avoid tankmates with incompatible thermal requirements.
breedingNotes: Breeding Scleromystax barbatus is achievable in captivity but requires conditioning and a temperature-drop trigger. Bring a well-conditioned group to spawning condition with protein-rich live and frozen foods, then perform a partial water change with slightly cooler water to simulate seasonal rainfall/temperature drop — a drop of 2–4°C is often sufficient. Eggs are adhesive and scattered on plants, glass, and substrate. Adults may consume eggs; spawning mops or fine-leaved plants provide some cover. Remove eggs to a separate container with gentle aeration for best hatch rates. Eggs hatch in approximately 4–6 days at 22°C. Fry accept micro-worms and freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii from free-swimming stage. Sexing adults is straightforward: males develop pronounced bristle-like odontodes on the cheeks and body flanks at maturity.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Scleromystax barbatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size, temperature range (18–24°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness, native range (southeastern Brazil coastal drainages), biotope, diet, sexing criteria (male odontodes), breeding trigger (cool water change), taxonomy note (Britto 2003 reclassification from Corydoras to Scleromystax), and grouping/compatibility guidance.

additional.name: FishBase — Scleromystax barbatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 12.5 cm TL), temperature range, native country (Brazil), taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes), and synonym Corydoras barbatus confirming pre-2003 classification.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Corydoras Care Guide
additional.notes: General corydoras husbandry guidance used to supplement fine-sand substrate requirement, group size (minimum 5–6), sinking food feeding practices, and barbel health considerations. Aquarium Co-Op does not carry a species-specific Scleromystax barbatus page; general corydoras guidance applied where consistent with species-specific sources.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Scleromystax barbatus profile
additional.notes: Supplementary source for cool-water preference confirmation, sexual dimorphism (male bristles/odontodes on operculum), minimum tank size, and breeding conditioning notes. Confirmed southeastern Brazilian coastal stream origin and preference for well-oxygenated, moderate-current setup.

additional.name: Britto, M.R. (2003) — Phylogenetic analysis of the subfamily Corydoradinae (Teleostei: Siluriformes: Callichthyidae)
additional.notes: Taxonomic authority for the reclassification of barbatus from Corydoras to the resurrected genus Scleromystax. Cited in scientificName notes to document the genus separation. Britto (2003) Zootaxa 214:1–96.

fish.breedingNotes: Conditional spawning triggered by cooler water change (2–4°C drop) after conditioning on live/frozen foods. Egg scatterer; adults may eat eggs — relocate to separate tank for best yield. Fry accept micro-worms and brine shrimp nauplii.
